author: @Buzzhumma
text:
Out of interest i had a mate who was a welder on the submarines built here in Australia and every weld had to be x-ray proofed. He ended up having to quit after 2 years as it was declining his mental health. He knew the weld was by far good enough and fit for purpose but he finally spit the dummy when they knocked back a seam weld on a standard hand rail in the kitchen area. No wonder these government department get charged 20 times more than a commercial private arrangement! They cant see the woods from the trees! The point being with welding is that the main consideration is the concept of fit for purpose.    
Not many welds have to be x-rayed .And most welding has to be ground regardless as all welds need to be prepared for coat for oxidation protection.

author: @tinkmarshino
text: I always collect all the parts then build the project.. That's how I learned is as a lad.. That was how I built as an adult and that is how I do it now.. that way when I start a project I can see it through to the end with out any delays and put it right in service.. I am not so big on esthetics but I do cut, drill, weld and fabricate precisely . But I guess that is because I have been a carpenter/woodworker all my life.. Watching your videos I see the project, draw it out on paper, think about it a bit, then when your build is all done I gather my parts and then build my version of it...... This is a very good video.. (not for fellas like me that have been building, welding and hammering things into existence for ever.). for those that are just starting out in there diy style of life.. Sometime I forget how frustrating it was back in the begging.. to see fellas just grab a bunch of scrap and hammer out a machine they needed. that is why I never made a good teacher.. even though when I had my construction company I always had others teach the new guys.. I had been doing it so long that I forgot that not every one knows the basics.. any way great job Rob! Carry on?

author: @MrTubeuser12
text: I think doing these projects in "real time" as it were, is the better way to do it. too many videos use "the magic of you tube" and it's all done lol. then when you try to do the same, it's like, hang on, it only took him 4 hours, why is it taking me 2 weeks ? and I think some people give up, good to see these projects play out as they happen :) BTW I see poor twizzy gathering dust :(, never mind, I'm sure you will get back to that project at some point :) also, with the grinding etc, nothing wrong with taking pride in your work and spending time to make the finished job look good, nicely done.

author: @jonnyswalk_7
text:
Great video Robert 👍 .
You can really the Wallace & Gromit/Steampunk influence on your build 🔧 and it looks 👀 great 👍 
Also I’d like to thank you again for your helpful explanations and wise words. 
You’re right this is a learning channel and we live in an instant gratification society. 
Whereas when you’re making things from things that you find ( you do have a gift for it mind you - brings to mind  the “Wombles” kids programme that I’d watch as a young un 😉 ) then it does take time. But I do like the “necessity being the mother of invention” & “using what I have” effect on the designs for that reason (& they always look good)
Anyway, Thanks again for all your efforts Robert - you’re doing a good 👏 thing and we’re all the beneficiaries of it 👍 So Thank you 😊 👍 
Warmest wishes from South Wales 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁷󠁬󠁳󠁿 - Jonathan 😊 👍 
P.s please pass on my best wishes to Luke also - He’s doing ever so well 👍

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text:
All you can do with the sealer is to try it, I used it years ago and it just was not up to it, however, it might be improved by now, they do make improvements to this sort of stuff all the time.  I did think of the exhaust sealer but it is a bit messy and you do need heat to make it dry and seal.  All good points about you videos and gaps between doing things, but if it was done all at once it would be far to long, plus, people would get fed up watching it, so I think you have got your video length down to about right.
You have always got something else going on anyway to break things up a bit.  Just keep doing what you are doing, you are doing all the right things and making me think outside the box for sure!

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I've been very appreciative of how you approach your videos specifically because you take us through the entire process.  Proper "experience" isn't confined to just perfect solutions and the most efficient ways of doing something, but also includes all the errors, obstacles, time issues, material constraints, failures, etc.  If you only gave us the post-success summary and condensed step-by-step, all we'd learn is how to imitate what you did.  One has to experience a lot of trouble before one can properly troubleshoot. 😊

author: @mikebond6328
text: 6013 is an all purpose mild steel rod. 7014 is the easiest to get started for beginners and it also deposits more metal than other rods so good for filling. The numbers of the rod are a code with the first two numbers denoting the tensil strength of the deposited material in 10,000 psi. So 6013 is rated at 60,000 psi and 7014 is rated at 70,000 psi. Mild steel, also known as A36 is rated at 36,000 psi meaning the weld is stronger than the steel which is why when a welded piece of equipment breaks it breaks beside the weld. Just a bit of interesting but mostly useless information.

author: @flashlit123
text: There is a technique I learned as Texas TIG where you use the stick welder with 2 rods.  One creates the arc and the second one is used as a filler rod kind of like a real TIG welder.  I used it when welding very thin sheet metal and I couldn't keep from burning through the metal.  The second rod added more metal and took some of the heat away from the thin metal.  It isn't a "professional" method and not for critical welds but it got the job done with the tools I had available.

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Hi Robert, one thing you should look at doing now is making your grate shaker so that you can automate it. You dont need to do this now but it should be designed so you can add this later. You will need this automated at some point. You can use a simple timer board or simply use an Arduino. I can help you with code if needed. Just use the blink with out delay sample and modify it. You maybe ok with the hopper since this is a small unit and assuming you are going to use pellet fuel. But generally on chip and chunk fuel machines you will need a hopper vibrator run on a timer to keep the fuel free and flowing. I also have code for an electronic air fuel mixture system along with a tutorial on how to build it. I will post a link here under this comment.
